watchFor: AVOID: Feb 10–13: four consecutive losing days totaling -$10,430 — that is a 10.3% drawdown on the day-trade account in four sessions. A hard daily loss limit rule must be enforced.
AVOID: Feb 11: 0-for-3 on the day, -$4,757. Three losses in one session without stopping is a major discipline failure.
AVOID: Feb 12: 2-for-6 wins, -$845 — six trades on a losing day signals overtrading and refusal to accept a bad day early.
AVOID: VIX data is missing for several recent days, suggesting the regime-detection system has gaps that may be contributing to poor entry timing.
tomorrowRules: Define your market regime before placing any order. If regime reads NEUTRAL or CHOPPY, reduce max trade count to 2 and cut size by 50%.
Hard stop: if first trade of the day is a loss and exceeds 0.5% of account ($452), close the platform and do not re-enter until next session.
Maximum 3 trades per day until win rate climbs above 45% over a rolling 20-trade sample.
Before entry, write down your invalidation level. If price hits it, exit — no averaging down, no hope trades.
Do not take a new trade within 15 minutes of a closed loss. Reset, review, then decide.

dailyLessons.gradingReason: No trades taken today in a NEUTRAL market regime. Sitting on hands when conditions don't favor your edge is disciplined, not lazy. However, five consecutive zero-trade days raises a question: is this patience or avoidance? The grade holds at B because capital was preserved.
keyLessons: No-trade days in choppy/neutral regimes ARE a valid outcome — your job is to protect the $90K day-trade account, not force activity.

Your cumulative win rate of 37.9% across 29 trades demands that your winners dwarf your losers. Feb 6 (+$20,632) proves you can do it, but Feb 10–13 (-$10,430 combined) shows your losses can cascade badly.
Five days of zero day-trading activity in a row suggests either a lack of qualifying setups (good) or a confidence dip after the Feb 10–13 drawdown streak (needs honest self-assessment).

With 18 losses versus 11 wins, your positive cumulative PnL (+$11,947) is entirely dependent on a small number of large wins. One bad sizing day can erase that edge — size discipline is non-negotiable.

goodPatterns: Recognized NEUTRAL regime and did not force trades — this aligns with a regime-filtered strategy.
No revenge trading following the Feb 10–13 losing streak; you stopped rather than doubled down.
Cumulative PnL is positive despite a sub-40% win rate, indicating you are cutting losers and letting winners run at least some of the time.
badPatterns: Feb 10–13: four consecutive losing days totaling -$10,430 — that is a 10.3% drawdown on the day-trade account in four sessions. A hard daily loss limit rule must be enforced.
Feb 11: 0-for-3 on the day, -$4,757. Three losses in one session without stopping is a major discipline failure.
Feb 12: 2-for-6 wins, -$845 — six trades on a losing day signals overtrading and refusal to accept a bad day early.
VIX data is missing for several recent days, suggesting the regime-detection system has gaps that may be contributing to poor entry timing.

tomorrowRules: Define your market regime before placing any order. If regime reads NEUTRAL or CHOPPY, reduce max trade count to 2 and cut size by 50%.
Hard stop: if first trade of the day is a loss and exceeds 0.5% of account ($452), close the platform and do not re-enter until next session.
Maximum 3 trades per day until win rate climbs above 45% over a rolling 20-trade sample.
Before entry, write down your invalidation level. If price hits it, exit — no averaging down, no hope trades.

dailyLessons.riskAssessment: Today: risk was perfectly managed — zero exposure, zero loss. Cumulative risk management is a serious concern. The Feb 10–13 streak represents unacceptable drawdown for a four-day window. A maximum daily loss rule of 1–1.5% of account equity ($900–$1,350) was clearly not enforced on Feb 11 (-$4,757 = ~5.3% of current equity). This must be addressed structurally, not just mentally.
dailyLessons.emotionalCheck: Today shows no emotional trading. The more important flag is the cluster of overtrading on bad days (Feb 11: 3 trades all losers, Feb 12: 6 trades mostly losers, Feb 13: 2 trades following a -$4,757 day). This pattern — continuing to trade aggressively after early losses — is a textbook revenge-trading signature. The current 5-day pause may reflect healthy restraint or emotional shutdown; journal your internal state to distinguish between the two.
